info.title: Triton API
info.description: Triton API - public-facing HTTP API for the Triton datacenter

get.summary: RFC 7517 JWKS document containing the public key(s) used to sign
get.description: access tokens. Consumed by external JWT verifiers -- the gateway today, any future adminui proxy or DC component tomorrow. No auth required.

post.summary: Authenticate an LDAP user and either issue tokens directly or
post.description:
require a second factor.

The response body is a tagged [`LoginOutcome`]:

* `complete` — password was correct and the user has no 2FA enrolment; the embedded fields are identical to the historical [`LoginResponse`] shape and a `Set-Cookie` header carries the access token for browser clients. * `challenge_required` — password was correct but the user has a second factor enrolled; the embedded [`LoginChallenge`] carries a `challenge_token` and the list of methods the client may use. The client must POST the `challenge_token` plus a code to `/v1/auth/login/verify`. No cookie is set on this branch since the session has not been established yet.

post.summary: Complete a 2FA login by presenting the challenge token and a
post.description:
second-factor code.

Called only when `/v1/auth/login` returned a `challenge_required` outcome. The server re-reads the user's TOTP secret from UFDS (it is never carried in the challenge), verifies the code, and returns the standard [`LoginResponse`] — same shape, same `Set-Cookie` semantics — that `/v1/auth/login` issues for non-2FA users.

post.summary: Exchange a proof-of-SSH-key-ownership for an access + refresh
post.description:
token pair. The caller presents an `Authorization: Signature …` header (draft-cavage HTTP Signature, same dialect cloudapi accepts). The server resolves the key via mahi, verifies the signature, and issues tokens via the same path the password login uses.

Request body is empty — all auth material is in the headers. Response mirrors `POST /v1/auth/login`.

post.summary: Revoke all outstanding refresh tokens for the caller and clear
post.description: the auth cookie. Accepts an expired access token so that callers whose session has already expired can still log out cleanly.

post.summary: Rotate a refresh token: consume the caller's refresh token and
post.description: return a new `(access, refresh)` pair. The old refresh token is single-use and is invalidated on success.

get.summary: Return the authenticated caller's identity, derived from the JWT
get.description: claims. Useful for web UIs to check login state on page load.

get.summary: List the authenticated caller's cluster records.
get.description:
Filtered server-side to records owned by the caller's account; no cross-account leakage.

Accepts Bearer JWT or HTTP Signature authentication.

post.summary: Create a Kelp-managed Kubernetes cluster record.
post.description:
Allocates the cluster identifier and stores a record owned by the authenticated caller. No Triton VMs are provisioned by this endpoint — bootstrap (a future endpoint) is responsible for allocating the fabric, provisioning control plane / worker VMs, and delivering Talos machine configs.

Accepts Bearer JWT or HTTP Signature authentication.

get.summary: Fetch a single cluster record by identifier.
get.description:
Returns 404 if the cluster does not exist or is owned by a different account (the two cases are intentionally indistinguishable to avoid leaking the existence of other accounts' clusters).

Accepts Bearer JWT or HTTP Signature authentication.

delete.description:
In Phase 1 this only removes the server-side record; once bootstrap exists, deletion will also tear down provisioned VMs and fabric resources. Returns 404 with the same indistinguishable-not-found semantics as [`Self::k8s_clusters_get`].

Accepts Bearer JWT or HTTP Signature authentication.

post.summary: Bootstrap a cluster: apply Talos configs, bootstrap etcd, retrieve
post.description:
kubeconfig.

The cluster must be in `created` state. Nodes listed in the request must already be running the relay agent so the server can reach them through the registered relay tunnel.

Returns 202 Accepted with the cluster record in `provisioning` state. Poll `GET /v1/k8s/clusters/{cluster}` until `state == "running"`.

Accepts Bearer JWT or HTTP Signature authentication.

get.summary: Retrieve the kubeconfig for a running cluster.
get.description:
Returns 404 if the cluster does not exist, is owned by a different account, or has not yet completed bootstrap (kubeconfig not yet available — the cluster is still `provisioning`).

Accepts Bearer JWT or HTTP Signature authentication.

post.summary: Add nodes to a running cluster.
post.description:
Each node must already have the relay agent active. The server applies the supplied Talos machine config in maintenance mode and triggers a reboot; the node then joins the existing cluster automatically. Control-plane joiner configs and worker configs are both accepted — role assignment is determined by the config content, not enforced server-side.

Returns 202 Accepted immediately. The node inventory in the cluster record is updated once configs have been applied.

Accepts Bearer JWT or HTTP Signature authentication.

post.summary: Trigger a rolling Talos OS upgrade across all cluster nodes.
post.description:
The cluster must be in `running` state. Control-plane nodes are upgraded first (sequentially to preserve etcd quorum), then worker nodes. The `talos_version` field in the cluster record is updated once all nodes have been upgraded.

Returns 202 Accepted immediately. Poll `GET /v1/k8s/clusters/{cluster}` to observe the updated `talos_version` when the upgrade completes.

Accepts Bearer JWT or HTTP Signature authentication.

get.description:
Called by the gateway-zone agent. The connection is upgraded to WebSocket and then to a yamux session (server = agent, client = API server). The API server opens yamux streams when bridge clients request connections; the agent dials the target on the fabric network and bridges bytes.

No authentication in the POC — this is intentional.

get.summary: Connect to the relay as a bridge client.
get.description:
Called by `triton-relay-bridge`. The connection is upgraded to WebSocket and then to a yamux session (server = API server, client = bridge). For each inbound stream from the bridge the API server opens a yamux stream to the registered agent and splices the two together.

No authentication in the POC — this is intentional.

AddNodesRequest.description:
Body of `POST /v1/k8s/clusters/{cluster}/nodes`.

Each node must already have the relay agent running. The server applies the supplied Talos machine config in maintenance mode and triggers a reboot; the node then joins the existing cluster automatically.

BootstrapClusterRequest.description:
Body of `POST /v1/k8s/clusters/{cluster}/bootstrap`.

The server provisions VMs via the operator CloudAPI client, generates all Talos PKI and machine configs, applies them through the relay tunnel, and retrieves the kubeconfig — all asynchronously.

Poll `GET /v1/k8s/clusters/{cluster}` until `state == "running"`.

control_plane_count.description: Number of control-plane nodes to provision. Must be at least 1. Use 3 for a highly-available control plane.

image.description: CloudAPI image name or UUID (must be a Talos nocloud image). Defaults to `"talos-1.12-nocloud"`.

install_disk.description: Disk to install Talos onto on each node. Defaults to `"/dev/sda"`.

package.description: CloudAPI package name or UUID for each node VM. Defaults to `"sample-2G"`.

talos_version.description: Talos installer image tag, e.g. `"v1.12.7"`. Defaults to `"v1.12.7"`.

worker_count.description: Number of worker nodes to provision. May be 0.

ChallengeMethod.description:
Second-factor authentication method offered for a login challenge.

Forward-compatible: clients deserialising an older binary can receive new method names from a newer server and round-trip them through `Unknown` rather than failing.

oneOf.description: Time-based one-time password (RFC 6238). Currently the only method tritonapi issues challenges for.
Catch-all for forward compatibility; an unrecognised method from a newer server.

Cluster.description:
A Kelp-managed Kubernetes cluster record (public API view).

The record exists independent of any provisioned VMs — `Created` state means "we know about this cluster but have not bootstrapped it yet." Bootstrap transitions the record through `Provisioning` to `Running`.

account_id.description: Owning Triton account UUID. Derived from the authenticated caller at create time; cannot be set by the client.

control_plane_count.description: Number of control-plane nodes currently tracked.

endpoint.description: Kubernetes API server endpoint URL. `None` until the control plane is operational.

fabric_network_id.description: Triton fabric network the cluster nodes are provisioned on. `None` until set at bootstrap time.

kubernetes_version.description: Target Kubernetes version (e.g. `1.30.3`). `None` until bootstrap begins selecting images.

state.description: Lifecycle state of the cluster record.

talos_version.description: Target Talos Linux version (e.g. `1.7.6`). `None` until bootstrap begins.

worker_count.description: Number of worker nodes currently tracked.

ClusterList.description:
Response body for `GET /v1/k8s/clusters`.

A wrapper struct (rather than a bare `Vec<Cluster>`) so future pagination metadata can be added without breaking the wire shape.

ClusterState.description:
Lifecycle state of a [`Cluster`] record.

Forward-compatible: clients deserialising an older binary can receive new state names from a newer server and round-trip them through `Unknown` rather than failing.

oneOf.description: Record exists, no VMs provisioned. `POST .../bootstrap` (future) transitions out of this state.
Bootstrap completed; the cluster is operational.
Cluster is reachable but at least one node or control-plane component is unhealthy.
Deletion is in progress; the record will disappear when the underlying VMs have been destroyed.
Bootstrap failed with an unrecoverable error; manual intervention (delete and re-create) is required.
Catch-all for forward compatibility; an unrecognised state name from a newer server.

CreateClusterRequest.description:
Body of `POST /v1/k8s/clusters`.

The server assigns `id`, `account_id`, `state`, and `created_at`. Version selection and network assignment happen at bootstrap time.

x.description: Base64url-encoded X coordinate of the EC public key.

y.description: Base64url-encoded Y coordinate of the EC public key.

JwkSet.description:
RFC 7517 JWKS document returned by `GET /v1/auth/jwks.json`.

Verifiers (triton-gateway, future admin-UI proxies, etc.) fetch this to obtain the public key(s) used to validate access tokens. Only ES256 P-256 keys are currently issued.

KubeconfigResponse.description:
Response body for `GET /v1/k8s/clusters/{cluster}/kubeconfig`.

The `kubeconfig` field is a complete YAML kubeconfig document. Callers can write it to `~/.kube/config` or merge it with an existing config via `kubectl config view --merge`.

LoginOutcome.description:
Outcome of `POST /v1/auth/login`.

Tagged on the wire by the `outcome` field. The common case is `complete`: the password verified, no second factor is enrolled, and the response carries the same fields a non-2FA `LoginResponse` always has. Users enrolled in 2FA receive `challenge_required`, must read a code from their authenticator, and post it together with the `challenge_token` to `/v1/auth/login/verify` to obtain a `LoginResponse`.

oneOf.description: Authentication is complete; tokens are issued.

refresh_token.description: Single-use refresh token; present it to `POST /v1/auth/refresh` for a new (token, refresh_token) pair.

oneOf.description: Authentication needs a second factor before tokens are issued. Carry the `challenge_token` to `/v1/auth/login/verify`.

challenge_token.description: Short-lived signed token. The client posts this verbatim alongside the second-factor code to `/v1/auth/login/verify`. Treat as opaque — the token's claims and TTL are server-side implementation details.

methods.description: Methods the client may use to satisfy the challenge. v1 only emits `[totp]`, but clients should tolerate additional values via the `Unknown` variant rather than failing closed.

LoginResponse.description: `POST /v1/auth/login` response body when the password is correct and no second factor is required. Also the response body of `POST /v1/auth/login/verify` (which always completes a session).

refresh_token.description: Single-use refresh token; present it to `POST /v1/auth/refresh` for a new (token, refresh_token) pair.

challenge_token.description: The exact `challenge_token` value from the `ChallengeRequired` outcome. Treated as opaque.

code.description: 6-digit code from the user's authenticator app.

NodeBootstrapRole.description: Role a node plays within a Kelp cluster.

NodeBootstrapSpec.description: Specification for a single node during cluster bootstrap or scale-out.

fabric_ip.description: Fabric IP of an already-running node that has the relay agent active. Example: `"10.0.0.5"`.

role.description: Role this node will play in the cluster.

UpgradeClusterRequest.description:
Body of `POST /v1/k8s/clusters/{cluster}/upgrade`.

Triggers a rolling Talos OS upgrade across all nodes in the cluster. Control-plane nodes are upgraded first (one at a time to maintain etcd quorum), then worker nodes.

preserve.description: Preserve the ephemeral data partition across the upgrade. Default: `false` (data partition is wiped — correct for most upgrades).

talos_image.description: Talos installer image to upgrade to, e.g. `ghcr.io/siderolabs/talos:v1.8.0`.

UserInfo.description: Public profile of the authenticated user. `email`, `name`, and `company` are only populated from `/v1/auth/login` (which reads UFDS); the `/v1/auth/session` endpoint can only return fields that live in the JWT claims, which is why those fields are optional.
